settings.title: Whole-home renovation of a 1924 craftsman
settings.summary: New systems, a reworked floor plan, and a restored fireplace — a hundred-year-old house set up for its next fifty.
settings.description:
The house had been remuddled twice — dropped ceilings, carpet over fir floors, and a warren of small rooms at the back. The owners wanted it opened up without losing what made it a craftsman.

Back to the studs where it mattered

We took it back to the studs where it mattered: all-new electrical, plumbing, and heating, insulation the house never had, and a rear floor plan reworked around one long sightline from kitchen to garden.

A hundred-year-old house hides its problems well. Knob-and-tube wiring, a gravity-furnace conversion from the sixties, and galvanized supply lines all came out. What went back in is boring, modern, and permitted, which is exactly what systems should be.

Keeping the craftsman

The original fir floors came back from under the carpet, and the brick fireplace was repointed rather than replaced. The dropped ceilings came out to reveal the full room heights the house was built with.

Where the original trim had survived, we restored it; where it had not, our shop milled new casing to match the old profile. New work and old sit in the same rooms without announcing which is which.

Five months, one move

Five months, one family relocated to a rental we helped them schedule around, and a house that should now outlive everyone involved in renovating it.

Before the walls closed, we walked the owners through the house at rough-in so every outlet, valve, and light was where they wanted it rather than where a plan said. It is the cheapest hour a renovation ever buys.
